solution.p: We will factor y^{2}+3y-40 where a=1, b=3, and c=-40.
Mental Method: When we factor a polynomial in the form of x^2+bx+c, x^2+bxy+cy^2, or x^2y^2+bxy+c we can use mental math to factor. We need to find two numbers whose product is c, and whose sum is b.
For y^{2}+3y-40, since the product of 8 and -5 is -40, and the sum of 8 and -5 is 3, the factorization is \begin{aligned}[t]y^{2}+3y-40\amp =\left(y+8\right)\mathopen{}\left(y-5\right)\end{aligned}
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (1) \cdot (-40) = -40.

The number -40 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(-40)\amp -1(40)\amp 2(-20)\amp -2(20)
ewline4(-10)\amp -4(10)\amp 5(-8)\amp -5(8)
ewline\end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of 3, and this sum happens with (-5)+8=3 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]y^{2}+3y-40\amp =y^{2}-5y+8y-40
ewline \amp =y\mathopen{}\left(y-5ight)+8\mathopen{}\left(y-5ight)
ewline \amp =\left(y+8ight)\mathopen{}\left(y-5ight)\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in y^{2}+3y-40 into the top left and bottom right rectangles:
The first term's coefficient, 1, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(1)\amp \end{array}

The third term, -40, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(-40)\amp -1(40)\amp 2(-20)\amp -2(20)\newline4(-10)\amp -4(10)\amp 5(-8)\amp -5(8)\newline\end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with y^{2}+3y-40 by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:
Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]y^{2}+3y-40\amp =\left(y+8\right)\mathopen{}\left(y-5\right)\end{aligned}
